scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is an Athenian orator documenting contemporary Athenian legal practices and social norms regarding the protection of citizen youths.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech Against Timarchus was delivered in 345 BCE, reflecting the laws and legal actions available at that time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses the specific legal status of 'demosioi' (state slaves) within the Athenian judicial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech Against Timarchus was delivered in 345 BCE, describing the contemporary legal standing of state slaves.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dispute involves Athenian citizens and public slaves within the Athenian legal framework.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The legal conflict described occurred during the lifetime of Aeschines and was cited in his 345 BCE speech.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The narrative describes the intervention of an Athenian citizen (of the deme Cholargus) in an Athenian court to prevent wrongful enslavement.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines recounts this specific legal settlement in his speech delivered in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to specific Athenian laws read aloud in court concerning the conduct of Athenian citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: These laws were contemporary to Aeschines' prosecution of Timarchus in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This rule concerns the 'dokimasia' (scrutiny) of Athenian citizens and their rights in the Assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The trial of Timarchus in 345 BCE is the primary instance of this rule's enforcement described in the text.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'diapsephisis' (revision of citizen lists) was a specific administrative process of the Athenian demes.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: A major general revision of the citizen lists occurred in 346/5 BCE, immediately preceding Aeschines' speech.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text mentions a measure by Demophilus affecting the Athenian popular assembly and courts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Demophilus proposed the decree regarding citizen-list revisions and associated penalties around 346 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Adynatoi' (disabled) pension was a well-documented welfare institution of 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as an active, standing law in Athens at the time of his 345 BCE speech.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the transition of an Athenian youth to the citizen list and the resulting control over patrimony.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The legal events concerning Timarchus' inheritance were contemporary to the trial in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'apophora' system was a standard feature of Athenian slave-holding, allowing slaves limited economic autonomy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This economic arrangement was common in 4th-century BCE Athens during Aeschines' career.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The liturgy system (trierarchy and choregia) was the primary method for funding state needs through wealthy Athenian citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes these obligations as contemporary burdens for the wealthy in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'euthynai' (accountability audits) were a cornerstone of Athenian democratic governance.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes these audits as a current and standard part of the Athenian constitution in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The inner workings and expulsion procedures of the Boule (Senate) are described as part of Athenian civic life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines recounts a specific contemporary incident involving the expulsion and reinstatement of a Senate member.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The awarding of a crown/testimonial to the Senate by the Assembly was a specific Athenian political ritual.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the contemporary rewards for political service in Athens during the mid-4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The conflict between statutory law and jury practice described occurs within the Athenian court system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this contemporary legal anomaly in his speech from 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The status of free-birth vs. slave-birth was the fundamental criterion for Athenian citizenship being debated in these demes.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The case of Philotades is part of the wave of citizen-list revisions occurring around 346 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'pornikon telos' was a tax specific to the Athenian state, farmed out by the Senate.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines mentions the tax farmers' contemporary role in identifying professionals for revenue purposes in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule involves the exclusion of certain citizens from the Athenian Ekklesia (Assembly).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This restriction is the central legal argument of the prosecution of Timarchus in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines explicitly quotes Athenian law regarding the separation of slaves from the cultural and physical space of the gymnasium.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as a standing law in the mid-4th century BCE to illustrate the distinction between slave and free.
